subject: Physics
q: A cylinder with adiabatic walls is closed at both ends and is divided into two compartments by a frictionless adiabatic piston. Ideal gas is filled in both (left and right) the compartments at same $P, V, T$. Heating is started from left side until pressure changes to $\dfrac{27P}{8}$. If initial volume of each compartment was $9$ litres then the final volume in right-hand side compartment is __________ litres. (for this ideal gas $C_P/C_V = 1.5$)
question: A cylinder with adiabatic walls is closed at both ends and is divided into two compartments by a frictionless adiabatic piston. Ideal gas is filled in both (left and right) the compartments at same $P, V, T$. Heating is started from left side until pressure changes to $\dfrac{27P}{8}$. If initial volume of each compartment was $9$ litres then the final volume in right-hand side compartment is __________ litres. (for this ideal gas $C_P/C_V = 1.5$)
solution:
The right compartment is enclosed by adiabatic walls and an adiabatic piston, and no heat is supplied to it. Therefore, the gas in the right compartment undergoes an adiabatic compression.

Since the piston is frictionless, the pressure in the right compartment is always equal to the pressure in the left compartment. The final pressure in both compartments is $P_f = \dfrac{27P}{8}$.

For the adiabatic process in the right compartment, the equation is:
$P_i V_i^\gamma = P_f V_f^\gamma$

Given $P_i = P$, $V_i = 9$ L, $P_f = \dfrac{27P}{8}$, and $\gamma = \dfrac{C_P}{C_V} = 1.5 = \dfrac{3}{2}$. Substituting these values:
$P(9)^{3/2} = \left(\dfrac{27P}{8}\right) V_f^{3/2}$

Since $9^{3/2} = (3^2)^{3/2} = 3^3 = 27$, the equation becomes:
$27P = \dfrac{27P}{8} V_f^{3/2}$

Dividing both sides by $27P$:
$1 = \dfrac{1}{8} V_f^{3/2}$

$V_f^{3/2} = 8$

Taking the power of $2/3$ on both sides:
$V_f = (8)^{2/3} = (2^3)^{2/3} = 2^2 = 4$ litres.

Answer: $4$

subject: Physics
q: 10 mole of oxygen is heated at constant volume from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The change in the internal energy of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ cal. (The molecular specific heat of oxygen at constant pressure, $C_{P}=7 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.)
question: 10 mole of oxygen is heated at constant volume from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The change in the internal energy of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ cal. (The molecular specific heat of oxygen at constant pressure, $C_{P}=7 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.)
solution:
Given: $n = 10$ mol,
$\Delta T = 40 - 30 = 10°C$,
$C_P = 7$ cal/mol·°C,
$R = 2$ cal/mol·°C
For ideal gas: $C_V = C_P - R = 7 - 2 = 5$ cal/mol·°C
Change in internal energy: $\Delta U = nC_V\Delta T = 10 \times 5 \times 10 = 500$ cal

subject: Physics
q: Consider a spherical shell of radius R at temperature T. The black body radiation inside it can be considered as an ideal gas of photons with internal energy per unit volume <math><mi>u</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>∝</mo><msup><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math> and pressure <math><mi>p</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . If the shell now undergoes an adiabatic expansion the relation between T and R is:
question: Consider a spherical shell of radius R at temperature T. The black body radiation inside it can be considered as an ideal gas of photons with internal energy per unit volume <math><mi>u</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>∝</mo><msup><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math> and pressure <math><mi>p</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . If the shell now undergoes an adiabatic expansion the relation between T and R is:
solution:
∵  in an adiabatic process.

Q=0

So by first law of thermodynamics

Q=dU+W

⇒0=dU+W

⇒W=-dU

∴W=PdV

⇒PdV=-dU ....(i)

Given that UV∝T4⇒U=kVT4

⇒dU=kdVT4=KT4dV+4T3V dT

Also, P=13UV=13kVT4V=KT43 

Putting these values in equation (i)

⇒KT43dV=-kT4dV+4T3V dT

⇒TdV3=-TdV-4VdT

⇒4T3dV=-4VdT

⇒13dVV=-dTT

⇒13ln⁡V=-ln⁡T ⇒ln⁡V=ln⁡T-3

⇒VT3=constant

43πR3 T3=constant

RT=constant

⇒T∝1R

subject: Physics
q: The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>moles</mi></math> of a rigid diatomic gas from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> when no work is done is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi></math> is the universal gas constant)
question: The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>moles</mi></math> of a rigid diatomic gas from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> when no work is done is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi></math> is the universal gas constant)
solution:
ΔQ=ΔU+ΔW
Here ΔW=0
ΔQ=ΔU=nCVΔT
ΔQ=4×5R2(50)=500R
Hence option (4).

subject: Physics
q:
Heat is given to an ideal gas in an isothermal process.

A. Internal energy of the gas will decrease.

B. Internal energy of the gas will increase.

C. Internal energy of the gas will not change.

D. The gas will do positive work.

E. The gas will do negative work.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Heat is given to an ideal gas in an isothermal process.

A. Internal energy of the gas will decrease.

B. Internal energy of the gas will increase.

C. Internal energy of the gas will not change.

D. The gas will do positive work.

E. The gas will do negative work.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
From the first law of thermodynamics, heat supplied to the system Q, change in internal energy of the system ∆U and work done by the system W are related as:

Q=∆U+W

Since the process is isothermal, the temperature of the system is constant and therefore ∆U=0 (i.e., internal energy is constant). 

⇒Q=W

As heat is supplied to the system, Q is positive and therefore W is positive i.e., gas will do positive work.

Hence, correct options are C and D.

subject: Physics
q: Two ideal Carnot engines operate in cascade (all heat given up by one engine is used by the other engine to produce work) between temperatures, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> . The temperature of the hot reservoir of the first engine is <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and the temperature of the cold reservoir of the second engine is <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> . <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is temperature of the sink of first engine which is also the source for the second engine. How is <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> related to <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , if both the engines perform equal amount of work?
question: Two ideal Carnot engines operate in cascade (all heat given up by one engine is used by the other engine to produce work) between temperatures, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> . The temperature of the hot reservoir of the first engine is <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and the temperature of the cold reservoir of the second engine is <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> . <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is temperature of the sink of first engine which is also the source for the second engine. How is <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> related to <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , if both the engines perform equal amount of work?
solution:
Q1: Heat input to Ist engine
Q: Heat rejected from Ist engine
Q2: Heat rejected from IInd engine
Work done by Ist engine = work done by IInd engine.
Q-Q2=Q1-Q
2Q=Q1+Q2
2=T1T+T2T
T=T1+T22

q: The internal energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>U</mi></mfenced></math>, pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>P</mi></mfenced></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> of an ideal gas are related as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>P</mi><mi>V</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The gas is
question: The internal energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>U</mi></mfenced></math>, pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>P</mi></mfenced></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> of an ideal gas are related as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>P</mi><mi>V</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The gas is
solution:
U=3PV+4

nf2RT=3PV+4

f2PV=3PV+4

f=6+8PV

Since degree of freedom is more than 6 therefore gas is polyatomic

subject: Physics
q: A given ideal gas with $\gamma=\frac{C_p}{C_v}=1.5$ at a temperature $T$. If the gas is compressed adiabatically to one-fourth of its initial volume, the final temperature will be
question: A given ideal gas with $\gamma=\frac{C_p}{C_v}=1.5$ at a temperature $T$. If the gas is compressed adiabatically to one-fourth of its initial volume, the final temperature will be
solution:
$T V^{\gamma-1}=$ constant
$$
\begin{aligned}
& T_1 V_1^{\gamma-1}=\mathrm{T}_2 V_2^{\gamma-1} \\
& \Rightarrow T(V)^{1 / 2}=\mathrm{T}_2\left(\frac{V}{4}\right)^{1 / 2} \\
& {\left[\because \gamma=1.5, T_1=T, V_1=V \text { and } V_2=\frac{V}{4}\right]} \\
& \therefore \quad T_2=\left(\frac{4 V}{V}\right)^{1 / 2} T=2 T
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
Match the CpCv ratio for ideal gases with different type of molecules:

Molecule Type Cp/Cv
(A) Monoatomic (I) 7/5
(B) Diatomic rigid molecules (II) 9/7
(C) Diatomic non-rigid molecules (III) 4/3
(D) Triatomic rigid molecules (IV) 5/3
question:
Match the CpCv ratio for ideal gases with different type of molecules:

Molecule Type Cp/Cv
(A) Monoatomic (I) 7/5
(B) Diatomic rigid molecules (II) 9/7
(C) Diatomic non-rigid molecules (III) 4/3
(D) Triatomic rigid molecules (IV) 5/3
solution:
Relation between the ratio of specific heat and degree of freedom is given by,

γ=1+2f  ...(1)

For monoatomic molecule, A, f=3

 ⇒γ=1+23=53

For diatomic rigid molecule B, f=5

 ⇒γ=1+25=75

For diatomic non-rigid molecule C, f=7 ⇒γ=1+27=97

For Triatomic rigid molecule D, f=6 ⇒γ=1+26=43

subject: Physics
q: The volume of an ideal gas $(\gamma=1.5)$ is changed adiabatically from 5 litres to 4 litres. The ratio of initial pressure to final pressure is:
question: The volume of an ideal gas $(\gamma=1.5)$ is changed adiabatically from 5 litres to 4 litres. The ratio of initial pressure to final pressure is:
solution:
For Adiabatic process
$\begin{aligned} & P_i V_i=P_f V_f^\gamma \\ & P_i(5)^{1.5}=P_f(4)^{1.5} \\ & \frac{P_i}{P_f}=\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{4}{5} \cdot\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{8}{5 \sqrt{5}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: One mole of diatomic ideal gas undergoes a cyclic process ABC as shown in figure. The process BC is adiabatic. The temperatures at A, B and C are 400 K, 800 K and 600 K respectively. Choose the correct statement :
question: One mole of diatomic ideal gas undergoes a cyclic process ABC as shown in figure. The process BC is adiabatic. The temperatures at A, B and C are 400 K, 800 K and 600 K respectively. Choose the correct statement :
options: The change in internal energy in whole cyclic process is 250 R
The change in internal energy in the process CA is 700 R.
The change in internal energy in the process AB is  - 350 R
The change in internal energy in the process BC is  - 500 R
solution:
Δ U CA = nc v T A - T C = 1 × 5 2 R - 2 0 0 = - 5 0 0 R

Δ U AB = nc v T B - T A = 1 × 5 2 R × 4 0 0 = + 1 0 0 0 R

Δ U BC = nc v T C - T B = 1 × 5 2 R - 2 0 0 = - 5 0 0 R

subject: Physics
q: An ideal gas initially at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ temperature, is compressed suddenly to one fourth of its volume. If the ratio of specific heat at constant pressure to that at constant volume is $3 / 2$, the change in temperature due to the thermodynamic process is _____ K.
question: An ideal gas initially at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ temperature, is compressed suddenly to one fourth of its volume. If the ratio of specific heat at constant pressure to that at constant volume is $3 / 2$, the change in temperature due to the thermodynamic process is _____ K.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \gamma=\frac{3}{2} \\ & \mathrm{Tv}^{\gamma-1}=\mathrm{C} \\ & 273 \mathrm{~V}_0^{0.5}=\mathrm{T}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{4}\right)^{0.5} \\ & \mathrm{~T}=273 \times 2=546 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{~T}=273\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A Carnot engine whose heat sinks at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, has an efficiency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. By how many degrees should the temperature of the source be changed to increase the efficiency by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the original efficiency ?
question: A Carnot engine whose heat sinks at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, has an efficiency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. By how many degrees should the temperature of the source be changed to increase the efficiency by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the original efficiency ?
solution:
Efficiency of carnot engine is given by, η=1-TsinkTsource.

For initial case: Tsink=27+273 °C=300 K

So we can write, 14=1-300T1⇒T1=400 K

Now for the second case: Tsink=300 K

After increasing the previous efficiency by 100%, value of efficiency will get doubled.

Therefore, 12=1-300T2 ⇒T2=600 K

Increase in temperature required will be, 600 K-400 K=200 K

subject: Physics
q: In thermodynamics, heat and work are :
question: In thermodynamics, heat and work are :
solution:
Heat and work are treated as path functions in thermodynamics.

ΔQ=ΔU+ΔW

Since work done by gas depends on type of process i.e. path and ΔU depends just on initial and final states, so ΔQ i.e. heat, also has to depend on process is path.

subject: Physics
q: In a carnot engine, the temperature of reservoir is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>527</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and that of sink is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. If the workdone by the engine when it transfers heat from reservoir to sink is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi></math>, the quantity of heat absorbed by the engine from reservoir is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: In a carnot engine, the temperature of reservoir is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>527</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and that of sink is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. If the workdone by the engine when it transfers heat from reservoir to sink is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi></math>, the quantity of heat absorbed by the engine from reservoir is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
Efficiency of Carnot engine is η=1-TLTH=WQ, where, Q is heat absorbed.

Here, TH=527+273=800 K

Now, η=1-200800=12Q

∴ 34=12000Q

∴ Q=16×106 J

subject: Physics
q:
A gas is kept in a container having walls which are thermally non-conducting. Initially the gas has a volume of $800 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ and temperature $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The change in temperature when the gas is adiabatically compressed to $200 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ is :
(Take $\gamma=1.5: \gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats at constant pressure and at constant volume)
question:
A gas is kept in a container having walls which are thermally non-conducting. Initially the gas has a volume of $800 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ and temperature $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The change in temperature when the gas is adiabatically compressed to $200 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ is :
(Take $\gamma=1.5: \gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats at constant pressure and at constant volume)
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}_1=800 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \quad \mathrm{~V}_2=200 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \\ & \mathrm{~T}_1=300 \mathrm{~K}
\end{aligned}$
for adiabatic
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{TV}^{\gamma-1}=\text { const. } \\ & (300)(800)^{1.5-1}=\mathrm{T}_2(200)^{1.5-1} \\ & \mathrm{~T}_2=300\left[\frac{800}{200}\right]^{0.5}=300 \times\left(2^2\right)^{1 / 2} \\ & \mathrm{~T}_2=600 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{~T}=600-300=300 \mathrm{~K}
\end{aligned}$

question: A certain amount of gas is taken through a cyclic process (A B C D A) that has two isobars, one isochore and one isothermal. The cycle can be represented on a $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{V}$ indicator diagram as :

subject: Physics
q: A Carnot engine operates between two reservoirs of temperatures <math><mn>900</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and <math><mn>300</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> . The engine performs <math><mn>1200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of work per cycle. The heat energy (in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> ) delivered by the engine to the low temperature reservoir, in a cycle, is _____________
question: A Carnot engine operates between two reservoirs of temperatures <math><mn>900</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and <math><mn>300</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> . The engine performs <math><mn>1200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of work per cycle. The heat energy (in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> ) delivered by the engine to the low temperature reservoir, in a cycle, is _____________
solution:
η=WQh=1-300900=23
Qh=32W=1800J
QL=Qh-W=600J

subject: Physics
q: A Carnot engine, whose efficiency is $40 \%$, takes in heat from a source maintained at a temperature of $500 \mathrm{~K}$ It is desired to have an engine of efficiency $60 \%$. Then, the intake temperature for the same exhaust (sink) temperature must be
question: A Carnot engine, whose efficiency is $40 \%$, takes in heat from a source maintained at a temperature of $500 \mathrm{~K}$ It is desired to have an engine of efficiency $60 \%$. Then, the intake temperature for the same exhaust (sink) temperature must be
options: efficiency of Carnot engine cannot be made larger than $50 \%$
solution:
$\frac{40}{100}=\frac{500-T_S}{500}, T_S=300 \mathrm{~K}$
$\frac{600}{100}=\frac{T-300}{T} \Rightarrow T=750 \mathrm{~K}$

subject: Physics
q: For a perfect gas, two pressures P1 and P2 are shown in figure. The graph shows
question: For a perfect gas, two pressures P1 and P2 are shown in figure. The graph shows
options: Insufficient data to draw any conclusion
solution:
We know that the ideal gas equation of gas is

PV=nRT

Keeping the temperature constant, we have, V∝1P

As seen from graph, V2>V1, so P1>P2.

subject: Physics
q: Work done by a Carnot engine operating between temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>127</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi></math>. The amount of heat transferred to the engine by the reservoir is:
question: Work done by a Carnot engine operating between temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>127</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi></math>. The amount of heat transferred to the engine by the reservoir is:
solution:
The temperatures in Kelvin scales are 

T2=27 °C+273=300 KT1=127 °C+273=400 K

The efficiency of the Carnot engine is

 η=1-T2T1=1-300400=14
Using the relation, η=WQ
⇒2 kJQ=14⇒Q=8 kJ

subject: Physics
q: An ideal gas undergoes a process maintaining relation between pressure $(P)$ and volume $(V)$ as $P = P_o\left(1 + \left(\dfrac{V_o}{V}\right)^2\right)^{-1}$, where $P_o$ and $V_o$ are constants. If two samples $A$ and $B$ (two moles each) with initial volumes $V_o$ and $3V_o$ respectively undergo above mentioned process and attain same pressure, then the difference at the temperatures of these samples, $T_B - T_A$ is _____. ($R = $ gas constant)
question: An ideal gas undergoes a process maintaining relation between pressure $(P)$ and volume $(V)$ as $P = P_o\left(1 + \left(\dfrac{V_o}{V}\right)^2\right)^{-1}$, where $P_o$ and $V_o$ are constants. If two samples $A$ and $B$ (two moles each) with initial volumes $V_o$ and $3V_o$ respectively undergo above mentioned process and attain same pressure, then the difference at the temperatures of these samples, $T_B - T_A$ is _____. ($R = $ gas constant)
solution:
The given relation between pressure and volume is:

$P = P_o\left(1 + \left(\dfrac{V_o}{V}\right)^2\right)^{-1}$

For sample A, the initial volume is $V_A = V_o$. Substituting this into the given relation, the initial pressure $P_A$ is:

$P_A = P_o\left(1 + \left(\dfrac{V_o}{V_o}\right)^2\right)^{-1} = \dfrac{P_o}{2}$

Using the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$ for $n = 2$ moles, the initial temperature $T_A$ is:

$T_A = \dfrac{P_A V_A}{nR} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{P_o}{2}\right) V_o}{2R} = \dfrac{P_o V_o}{4R}$

For sample B, the initial volume is $V_B = 3V_o$. Substituting this into the relation, the initial pressure $P_B$ is:

$P_B = P_o\left(1 + \left(\dfrac{V_o}{3V_o}\right)^2\right)^{-1} = P_o\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{9}\right)^{-1} = \dfrac{9P_o}{10}$

The initial temperature $T_B$ for $n = 2$ moles is:

$T_B = \dfrac{P_B V_B}{nR} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{9P_o}{10}\right) (3V_o)}{2R} = \dfrac{27P_o V_o}{20R}$

The difference in the initial temperatures of the two samples is:

$T_B - T_A = \dfrac{27P_o V_o}{20R} - \dfrac{P_o V_o}{4R}$

$T_B - T_A = \dfrac{27P_o V_o - 5P_o V_o}{20R} = \dfrac{22P_o V_o}{20R} = \dfrac{11P_o V_o}{10R}$

Answer: $\dfrac{11P_o V_o}{10R}$

q: The correct relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi></msub><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi></msub></mfrac></math> and temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is :
question: The correct relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi></msub><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi></msub></mfrac></math> and temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is :
solution:
The ratio of molar specific heat capacity at constant pressure to the molar specific heat capacity at constant volume is

CPCV=γ

where γ is constant for a given gas.

Therefore,

CPCV∝T0

q: An engine takes in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> moles of air at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>, and compresses it adiabatically to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> of the original volume. Assuming air to be a diatomic ideal gas made up of rigid molecules, the change in its internal energy during this process comes out to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>XkJ</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> to the nearest integer is:
question: An engine takes in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> moles of air at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>, and compresses it adiabatically to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> of the original volume. Assuming air to be a diatomic ideal gas made up of rigid molecules, the change in its internal energy during this process comes out to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>XkJ</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> to the nearest integer is:
solution:
T1V1γ-1=T2V2γ-1

T2=T1V1V2γ-1

=T1(10)75-1

T2=T1(10)2/5

ΔV=52nR;52×5×3102/5-1(293)

6256×1.5×293=461440

≈46ks

subject: Physics
q:
Consider the following statements:
A. Zeroth law of thermodynamics gives concept of temperature
B. First law of thermodynamics gives concept of internal energy
C. In isothermal expansion of ideal gas, $\Delta Q \neq \Delta W$
D. Product of intensive and extensive variables is extensive
E. The ratio of any extensive variable to mass will be an extensive variable
Choose the correct combination of statements from the options given below:
question:
Consider the following statements:
A. Zeroth law of thermodynamics gives concept of temperature
B. First law of thermodynamics gives concept of internal energy
C. In isothermal expansion of ideal gas, $\Delta Q \neq \Delta W$
D. Product of intensive and extensive variables is extensive
E. The ratio of any extensive variable to mass will be an extensive variable
Choose the correct combination of statements from the options given below:
solution:
Statement A is correct because the zeroth law of thermodynamics defines temperature and thermal equilibrium.

Statement B is correct because the first law of thermodynamics introduces the concept of internal energy through the equation $\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$.

Statement C is incorrect because for an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, the change in internal energy $\Delta U = 0$. According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta Q = \Delta W$.

Statement D is correct because if an intensive variable (which is independent of the size of the system) is multiplied by an extensive variable (which is directly proportional to the size of the system), the product will be directly proportional to the size of the system, making it an extensive variable.

Statement E is incorrect because the ratio of two extensive variables (like any extensive variable and mass) is independent of the size of the system, making it an intensive variable.

Therefore, only statements A, B, and D are correct.

Answer: A, B and D Only

subject: Physics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is the efficiency of an engine at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>447</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>147</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is the efficiency at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>947</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>47</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is the efficiency of an engine at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>447</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>147</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is the efficiency at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>947</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>47</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be
solution:
The efficiency of an engine is given by η=1-TLTH. Here, TL and TH are in Kelvin scale.

⇒η1=1-147+273447+273=1-420720

⇒η1=300720

And η2=1-47+273947+273=1-3201220

⇒η2=9001220

⇒η1η2=300720×1220900=12272×3

⇒η1η2=0.56

subject: Physics
q: A Carnot freezer takes heat from water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> inside it and rejects it to the room at a temperature of <math><msup><mrow><mn>27</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The latent heat of ice is <math><mn>336</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>J</mi><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If 5kg of water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> is converted into ice at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> by the freezer, then the energy consumed by the freezer is close to :
question: A Carnot freezer takes heat from water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> inside it and rejects it to the room at a temperature of <math><msup><mrow><mn>27</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The latent heat of ice is <math><mn>336</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>J</mi><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If 5kg of water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> is converted into ice at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> by the freezer, then the energy consumed by the freezer is close to :
solution:
Q=mL

=5×336×103=Qsink

QsinkQsource=TsinkTsource

∴   Qsource=TsourceTsinkQsink

∴   Work done =Qsource-Qsink

=QsinkTsourceTsink-1

=5×336×103300273-1

=1.67×105 J

q: A heat engine has an efficiency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>6</mn></mfrac></math>. When the temperature of sink is reduced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>62</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, its efficiency get doubled. The temperature of the source is :
question: A heat engine has an efficiency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>6</mn></mfrac></math>. When the temperature of sink is reduced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>62</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, its efficiency get doubled. The temperature of the source is :
solution:
η=1-TLTH ......(i)

2η=1-TL-62TH=1-TLTH+62 TH

⇒η=62 TH⇒16=62 TH⇒TH=6×62=372 K

In C∘⇒372-273=99°C

subject: Physics
q: In the reported figure, heat energy absorbed by a system in going through a cyclic process is ______πJ.
question: In the reported figure, heat energy absorbed by a system in going through a cyclic process is ______πJ.
solution:
For complete cyclic process
ΔU=0
∴ from ΔQ=ΔU+W
=0+W
ΔQ=W
=Area
=πr1·r2
=π×10×103×10×10-3
ΔQ=100π
∴ Answer =100
